name: Frontend System Design
description: Think like a senior — state management, API layers, caching, and designing scalable frontend systems.

cards.front: What is frontend system design? How is it different from backend system design?
cards.back:
Frontend system design = how you structure a client-side app at scale.

• Backend focuses on: databases, servers, queues, throughput
• Frontend focuses on: UI architecture, state flow, rendering, UX perf

Key frontend concerns:
• Component hierarchy & data flow
• State management strategy
• API integration layer
• Caching & optimistic UI
• Auth flows & route protection
• Error/loading states everywhere
• Bundle size & performance budgets

Think of it as: backend = how data is stored & served, frontend = how data is displayed & interacted with.

cards.front: What's the difference between client-side state and server state? Why does it matter?
cards.back:
Two different beasts — treat them differently.

• Client state: lives only in the browser
  → UI toggles, form inputs, modals open/closed, theme
  → You own it. It's the source of truth.

• Server state: originates from an API/database
  → User profile, list of posts, notifications
  → It's a cache of someone else's truth.

Why it matters:
• Server state needs syncing, refetching, invalidation
• Client state just needs local updates
• Mixing them (e.g. Redux for everything) causes unnecessary complexity

Modern approach:
• Client state → useState, Zustand, Context
• Server state → React Query, SWR, Apollo

Senior move: pick the right tool for each type.

cards.front: Compare state management approaches: local state, Context, Zustand, and Redux. When do you pick each?
cards.back:
Pick based on scope and complexity:

• Local state (useState/useReducer)
  → Component-specific UI state
  → Default choice. Start here.

• React Context
  → Shared state that rarely changes (theme, auth, locale)
  → ⚠️ Every consumer re-renders on any change

• Zustand
  → Lightweight global store, selective subscriptions
  → Great for: mid-size apps, replacing Context perf issues
  → Minimal boilerplate, no providers needed

• Redux (Toolkit)
  → Complex global state with strict patterns
  → Great for: large teams, time-travel debugging, middleware
  → Overhead justified only at scale

Senior rule of thumb:
• Don't reach for global state until local state hurts
• Separate server state (React Query) from UI state
• Zustand is the sweet spot for most apps today

cards.front: How should you design the API layer in a frontend app? Where does data fetching live?
cards.back:
Goal: isolate data fetching from UI components.

• Create an API layer (e.g. src/api/ or src/services/)
  → Thin wrappers around fetch/axios
  → Handles base URL, auth headers, error transforms

• Use a data-fetching library on top:
  → React Query / SWR for REST
  → Apollo / urql for GraphQL
  → Handles caching, retries, deduplication for free

Architecture pattern:
• api/client.ts → configured fetch instance
• api/users.ts → getUser(), updateUser() functions
• hooks/useUser.ts → useQuery wrapping api/users.ts
• Component → calls useUser(), never fetches directly

Why this matters:
• Swap backends without touching components
• Centralized error handling & auth refresh
• Easier testing — mock the API layer
• Components stay pure UI

cards.front: What caching strategies matter on the frontend? How do you keep data fresh?
cards.back:
Caching is the #1 perf lever on the frontend.

• HTTP caching (browser-level)
  → Cache-Control, ETag, stale-while-revalidate headers
  → Free speed — configure your CDN/server properly

• In-memory query cache (React Query/SWR)
  → staleTime: how long before data is considered stale
  → cacheTime: how long unused data stays in memory
  → Stale-while-revalidate: show cache, refetch in background

• Persistent cache (localStorage, IndexedDB)
  → Offline support, fast cold starts
  → ⚠️ Need invalidation strategy — stale data is dangerous

• Normalized cache (Apollo, Redux)
  → Store entities by ID, update everywhere at once
  → Great for relational data, overkill for simple apps

Key decisions:
• What's the staleness tolerance? (5s vs 5min vs forever)
• Does the user need offline access?
• How do you invalidate after mutations?

cards.front: What are optimistic updates? When should you use them?
cards.back:
Optimistic update = update the UI before the server confirms.

How it works:
• 1. User clicks 'Like'
• 2. Immediately show liked state (don't wait for API)
• 3. Send request to server in background
• 4. If it fails → roll back to previous state

When to use:
• Actions that almost always succeed (likes, toggles, reordering)
• Low-risk operations where rollback is simple
• When perceived speed matters for UX

When NOT to use:
• Payment/checkout flows — never fake success
• Complex operations with side effects
• When failure is common (bad network conditions)

Implementation with React Query:
• onMutate: save previous state, update cache
• onError: restore previous state from snapshot
• onSettled: refetch to ensure server truth

Senior insight: optimistic UI feels fast but adds complexity. Use for quick wins, not everywhere.

cards.front: How do you handle authentication and authorization in a single-page app?
cards.back:
Auth in SPAs has unique challenges — no server-rendered sessions.

Authentication (who are you?):
• Token-based: JWT stored in memory or httpOnly cookie
• ⚠️ Never store tokens in localStorage (XSS vulnerable)
• Best practice: httpOnly cookie + refresh token rotation
• Auth flow: login → get tokens → attach to API requests

Authorization (what can you do?):
• Route guards: check auth before rendering protected routes
• Component-level: conditionally render based on roles/permissions
• API-level: always enforce on the server too — frontend is just UX

Common architecture:
• AuthProvider context: holds user state + login/logout methods
• useAuth() hook: access user, isAuthenticated, permissions
• API interceptor: auto-attach token, handle 401 → refresh or redirect
• Protected route wrapper: redirect to /login if unauthenticated

Senior rule: frontend auth is UX, not security. Server must always validate.

cards.front: Offset pagination vs cursor pagination — what's the difference and when to use each?
cards.back:
Two ways to paginate API results:

• Offset-based: ?page=3&limit=20
  → Simple to implement and understand
  → User can jump to any page
  → ⚠️ Breaks with real-time data (items shift between pages)
  → ⚠️ Slow on large datasets (DB scans to offset)

• Cursor-based: ?after=abc123&limit=20
  → Cursor = pointer to a specific item (usually encoded ID + timestamp)
  → Stable results even with new inserts
  → Fast on large datasets (indexed lookup)
  → ⚠️ No random page access — only next/previous

When to use offset:
• Admin tables, dashboards, small datasets
• Users need 'go to page 5'

When to use cursor:
• Infinite scroll feeds (social, chat, activity)
• Real-time data that changes frequently
• Large datasets where performance matters

Senior tip: most modern APIs (GraphQL, Twitter, Slack) use cursor pagination for feeds.

cards.front: Real-time data on the frontend: WebSockets vs polling vs Server-Sent Events (SSE)?
cards.back:
Three approaches to getting live data:

• Polling (short polling)
  → Fetch every N seconds via setInterval
  → Simple, works everywhere
  → ⚠️ Wasteful if nothing changed, delayed updates
  → Good for: dashboards refreshing every 30s

• Long polling
  → Server holds request open until new data exists
  → Lower latency than short polling
  → ⚠️ Still one-directional, connection overhead

• Server-Sent Events (SSE)
  → Server pushes events over HTTP (EventSource API)
  → One-way: server → client only
  → Auto-reconnect built in, simple to implement
  → Good for: notifications, live scores, log streaming

• WebSockets
  → Full-duplex: both directions simultaneously
  → Lowest latency, most powerful
  → ⚠️ Complex: reconnection, auth, scaling
  → Good for: chat, collaborative editing, gaming

Decision framework:
• Need two-way? → WebSocket
• Server push only? → SSE (simpler) or WebSocket
• Simplicity over speed? → Polling

cards.front: How should you design an error handling strategy for a frontend app?
cards.back:
Errors will happen — plan for them at every layer.

• API errors (network / server):
  → Centralized in API layer: interceptors catch 4xx/5xx
  → Map to user-friendly messages
  → Auto-retry transient failures (503, network)
  → 401 → trigger token refresh or redirect to login

• UI rendering errors:
  → React Error Boundaries catch component crashes
  → Show fallback UI, not a white screen
  → Log to monitoring (Sentry, DataDog)

• Form validation errors:
  → Inline field-level validation + form-level summary
  → Show errors on blur or submit, not on every keystroke

• Global error state:
  → Toast/snackbar for transient errors (network hiccup)
  → Inline for contextual errors (form field invalid)
  → Full-page for fatal errors (app can't load)

Senior pattern:
• ErrorBoundary at route level + critical section level
• Centralized error logging service
• Never swallow errors silently — always log + inform user

cards.front: How do feature flags work on the frontend? Why use them?
cards.back:
Feature flags = toggles that control which features are active, without deploying new code.

How they work:
• 1. App fetches flags on init (from LaunchDarkly, Unleash, or your API)
• 2. Flags stored in context/global state
• 3. Components check: if (flags.newCheckout) → render new UI
• 4. Flags can target: % of users, specific users, environments

Why use them:
• Ship code to prod without exposing it (trunk-based dev)
• Gradual rollouts: 5% → 25% → 100%
• Kill switch: instant rollback without deploy
• A/B testing: compare two versions

Frontend implementation:
• FeatureFlagProvider wraps app, fetches flags
• useFeatureFlag('new-checkout') hook
• Fallback to default values if flag service is down

⚠️ Watch out for:
• Flag debt: clean up old flags or they pile up
• Combinatorial explosion: too many flags = untestable states
• Flickering UI: show loading state until flags are ready

Senior move: treat flags as temporary. Every flag has a removal date.

cards.front: System Design: How would you design a search/autocomplete component?
cards.back:
Classic frontend system design question.

Requirements to clarify:
• Client-side filtering or server search?
• How many items in the dataset?
• Need fuzzy matching? Highlighting?

Core architecture:
• Debounced input (300ms) to avoid flooding the API
• AbortController: cancel previous request when user types more
• Cache recent queries in memory (Map or React Query)
• Keyboard navigation: arrow keys + enter to select

API design:
• GET /search?q=react&limit=10
• Return: { results: [], total: number }
• Server should handle ranking/relevance

UX details that matter:
• Show loading spinner in dropdown, not full-page
• Highlight matching text in results
• Handle empty state: 'No results for X'
• Recent searches stored in localStorage
• Minimum 2-3 chars before searching

Performance:
• Virtualize long result lists (react-window)
• Prefetch popular queries if possible

Accessibility:
• combobox ARIA role, aria-activedescendant
• Screen reader announcements for result count

cards.front: System Design: How would you design an infinite scroll feed (like Twitter/Instagram)?
cards.back:
Key challenge: performance at scale with dynamic content.

Data fetching:
• Cursor-based pagination (not offset — items shift in feeds)
• Fetch next page when user is ~80% scrolled (IntersectionObserver)
• React Query useInfiniteQuery handles pages elegantly

Rendering:
• Virtualization is essential (react-window, react-virtuoso)
  → Only render visible items + small buffer
  → Without it: 1000 DOM nodes = laggy scrolling
• Each item needs a stable, unique key (not array index)

State management:
• Append new pages to existing list in cache
• Deduplicate items by ID (real-time feeds may have overlap)
• Pull-to-refresh: prepend new items or show 'New posts' banner

Edge cases:
• Loading state: skeleton cards, not a spinner
• Error on page 5: retry button, don't lose pages 1-4
• Empty feed: onboarding CTA, not blank screen
• Scroll restoration: save position on navigate away

Performance budget:
• < 100ms per page fetch
• < 16ms per frame while scrolling (60fps)
• Lazy load images with loading='lazy' or IntersectionObserver

cards.front: How should you approach a frontend system design interview?
cards.back:
Use a structured framework — don't just start coding.

• Step 1: Clarify requirements (2-3 min)
  → Who are the users? Scale? Core features vs nice-to-have?
  → Mobile? Offline? Accessibility? i18n?

• Step 2: High-level architecture (5 min)
  → Draw component tree / data flow diagram
  → Identify: pages, shared components, data sources
  → Choose rendering strategy: CSR, SSR, or hybrid

• Step 3: Data model & API design (5 min)
  → What entities? What endpoints?
  → State: what's client vs server state?
  → Caching & sync strategy

• Step 4: Deep dive on core feature (10-15 min)
  → Pick the hardest feature, design it thoroughly
  → Component breakdown, state flow, edge cases
  → Discuss tradeoffs explicitly

• Step 5: Performance & polish (5 min)
  → Bundle splitting, lazy loading, virtualization
  → Error handling, loading states, a11y
  → Monitoring & observability

Senior signals:
• You discuss tradeoffs, not just solutions
• You think about failure modes
• You mention testing strategy

cards.front: What are performance budgets? How do you monitor frontend performance?
cards.back:
Performance budget = a set of limits your app must stay within.

Common budgets:
• Bundle size: < 200KB JS (gzipped) for initial load
• Largest Contentful Paint (LCP): < 2.5s
• First Input Delay (FID): < 100ms
• Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS): < 0.1
• Time to Interactive (TTI): < 3.5s

How to enforce:
• CI checks: bundlesize, Lighthouse CI, size-limit
  → Fail the build if bundle exceeds budget
• Webpack/Vite: analyze with bundle-analyzer plugin
• Import cost: VS Code extension shows import sizes

Monitoring in production:
• Real User Monitoring (RUM): track actual user metrics
  → Tools: Datadog RUM, Sentry Performance, web-vitals library
• Synthetic monitoring: Lighthouse in CI, WebPageTest
• Custom marks: performance.mark() / performance.measure()

Senior approach:
• Set budgets early, not after things are slow
• Track Core Web Vitals by route, not just globally
• Budget per-route, not just total app
• Review perf metrics in sprint retros — treat like bugs
